2.7:
15. be not proud--

Pride was the cause of their contumacy, as humility is the first step to obedience ( Jr 13:17 ; Psa 10:4 ).
2.13:
20. from ... north--

Nebuchadnezzar and his hostile army ( Jr 1:14 ; 6:22). Jeremiah, amazed at the depopulation caused by Nebuchadnezzar's forces, addresses Jerusalem (a noun of multitude, which accounts for the blending of plural and singular, Your eyes ... thee ... thy flock), and asks where is the population ( Jr 13:17 , "flock") which God had given her?

3.1:
25. portion of thy measures--

the portion which I have measured out to thee ( Job 20:29 ; Psa 11:6 ). ( Jr 13:27 ), false gods and alliances with foreign idolaters.
3.7:
2. gates--

The place of public concourse in each city looks sad, as being no longer frequented ( Is 3:26 ; 24:4 ). that is, they mourn (blackness being indicative of sorrow), ( Jr 8:21 ). bowing towards it. of distress ( 1Sa 5:12 ; Is 24:11 ).
3.13:
7. do thou it--

what we beg of Thee; interpose to remove the drought. Jeremiah pleads in the name of his nation ( Psa 109:21 ). So "work for us," absolutely used ( 1Sa 14:6 ). "for our backslidings are so many" that we cannot urge Thee for the sake of our doings, but for the glory of Thy name; lest, if Thou give us not aid, it should be said it was owing to Thy want of power ( Jos 7:9 ; Psa 79:9 ; 106:8 ; Is 48:9 ; Eze 20:44 ). The same appeal to God's mercy, "for His name's sake," as our only hope, since our sin precludes trust in ourselves, occurs in Psa 25:11 .

4.6: 17. ( Jr 9:1 ; La 1:16 ). Jeremiah is desired to weep ceaselessly for the calamities coming on his nation (called a "virgin," as being heretofore never under foreign yoke), ( Is 23:4 ).
